w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Below the Concert: When a VTuber's Audience Is Thirty People

The global VTuber market is forecast to reach USD 13.62 billion by 2033, built on the parasocial bonds between distant idols and millions of fans. But the most intense version of that bond may happen far below the radar of any market forecast—in single rented rooms, between streamers and audiences small enough to know each other by name.
The expected increasing of Vtuber market size from 2025 to 2032,
It is 7:30 on a Tuesday evening when the pink-and-white cat girl appears on screen. She wears a pink night dress, has fluffy ears that tilt when she listens, and answers to the name

. Within seconds of her stream starting, twenty-five to thirty-five familiar usernames are typing greetings. Drink water tonight. Don't stream too late. She knows them by handle. She greets them back.

is in her mid-twenties. She designed the avatar herself, then asked a friend who paints to refine the sketches. The character is meant, she said in Mandarin, to feel like being wrapped in a warm blanket — soft

, head tilted toward whoever is speaking. The bravery, though, is borrowed from elsewhere. "She'll say things in the stream that I would never say to a stranger in real life," she said. "She'll tell people, '
Fine, we can just take it easy

She started streaming in late 2023, in a single rented room in a city where she had moved for a corporate job after university. Her parents wanted her to sit civil-service exams. She wanted, she said, to find people who liked the things she liked, in a face that wouldn't be disliked. For the first months she doubted herself daily. "I just wanted to try," she said. "At least if I tried, I wouldn't regret it."

She streams three or four nights a week. The regulars — about thirty of them — show up almost exactly on time. "Each time I open the stream, the familiar profile pictures light up one after another," she said. "Really like family."

The standard story about VTubers — the one that has reached English-language press — is about anonymity as distance. The animated avatar, the voice changing, the nakanohito (the "person inside") whose real face viewers will never

these create the conditions for what media researchers call parasocial relationships, one-way bonds with figures who do not know their fans exist. A 2025 study presented at CHI, the field's main academic conference, analyzed 13,655 Reddit posts about VTuber retirements and found that fans grieved retirements with reactions strikingly similar to those of bereavement — shock, numbness, and expressions of loyalty that persisted long after the streamer had gone.
But that research was built on the biggest names — performers whose final streams drew over half a million concurrent viewers. At the scale

works at, the equation inverts. Her thirty regulars know her stream times. They know which games make her laugh. The avatar is not creating distance. It is creating, paradoxically, the permission to come close.

who described himself as a DD — a fan who watches "anyone and everyone" — was specific about why he ended up sitting in small streams. "Giving small VTubers gifts has real emotional value," he said in Mandarin. "And because there aren't too many people, you won't get ignored." Asked why he stays in any particular streamer's
he was less able to explain. "Maybe I just like the personality," he said. "I'm a bit of a dead-inside type. Watching this kind of person feels more comfortable."
He keeps contact through Bilibili private messages and one QQ fan group, and only there. "My experience," he said, "is don't get too close. When she leaves, it won't hurt as much."
I have watched VTubers for nearly a
once tried streaming as one myself. The thing nobody tells you is how the smallness of the audience reshapes everything. A room of thirty does not behave like a room of three thousand.
The Chinese VTuber industry is, however, increasingly run by people who think in thousands.
more than 520,000 visitors. Its closest rival, Anycolor, parent of Nijisanji, came in just behind at ¥42.9 billion.
from USD 3.06 billion in 2025 to USD 13.62 billion
a compound rate of 20.5 per cent
The agencies that serve the Chinese-language part of that market mostly do not look like Cover. One of them is
, who asked not to be named, said the company currently has
," he said. "We don't chase numbers. We chase each person's long-term, healthy development."

e said, too many independent streamers in the boom years burn out at fifty to a hundred viewers because "they didn't know how to negotiate sponsorships, didn't know how to handle fan conflicts, and when something broke on the stream, nobody could fix it."

e is careful not to romanticize either path. Independence offers complete creative freedom and all the money. It also asks one person to be technician, accountant, lawyer and content creator. "Most independent streamers eventually burn out," he said. "But the top independent ones — those who have a stable audience and run themselves — can do better than people in mid-tier agencies. There is no absolute answer."
Another independent streamer, when asked what had surprised h

most about the work, gave a simpler answer. "Honestly, the broadcast length," he said. "When I was just watching, I didn't think it took this much time. Now that I'm streaming three or four hours myself, it can feel long." Asked whether what she does and what the agency-signed stars do are the same job, he was equally direct. "Of course not. Most big
companies have completely shifted to idol-style operations. The bottom and the top both look like Live2D streams, but they have almost nothing in common."
Stopping is quieter at this scale
When the biggest VTubers retire, they do it in front of half a million people. When the small ones stop, almost no one notices.

A former independent streamer, who ended her run at the close of 2024, described it in two sentences. "It was always a struggle. I had a full-time job, and the part-time streaming had started affecting my main work without producing results." Her boss had warned her at the office. That evening, she went on stream and felt, she said, like she was forcing a smile.
She held a graduation broadcast — the standard farewell ritual in Chinese-language VTuber circles. She did not upload the recording. "I was afraid Bilibili's
mechanic would talk me out of it," she said. The phrase, untranslatable cleanly, refers to the platform's habit of
retiring streamers in a way that can pull them back.

She kept the avatar. "It's still with me. If I run short on money one day, I might sell it." Asked whether she still thinks about the character, she paused. "Sometimes. It is a form I put my own effort into."

She does not compare any of this to the industry-level events that English-language press covers. "My stopping was normal," she said. "I just couldn't keep going. I love my fans, but I couldn't give them enough emotional value."
The Usual Desktop when streaming, including earphones/headphone, microphone, the iPhone required for face capturing through
will appear again. Twenty-five regulars will type drink water and don't stream too late. She will tilt her head when they speak. None of it will move a market forecast. None of it will be measured.

said, "is to keep this heart — the one that still wants to connect with people, still wants to glow. You deserve to be loved." She was answering a question about what she would say to her three-years-younger self. But by the end of the sentence it sounded as though she might also have been speaking to her regulars. Or to herself. The room is small enough that the distinction has stopped mattering.
